cheddar-cheese.reason: Contains lactose and is a dairy product; the file notes that some pets are lactose intolerant and may develop diarrhea, gas, or vomiting.
High in fat and calories; overfeeding can lead to weight gain.
High fat content can trigger pancreatitis, as noted in the file.
High in fat, which is inappropriate for pets with elevated blood lipids.
High in sodium; the file recommends choosing low-sodium varieties only.

cheese.reason: Dairy product containing lactose; the file states some dogs are lactose intolerant and cats may react.
High in fat and calories, making it a poor choice for overweight pets.
High fat content may trigger pancreatitis; the file lists pancreatitis-risk as a symptom.
High fat content can worsen hyperlipidemia.
High in salt; only low-sodium varieties are recommended.

chestnuts.reason: Starchy and calorie-dense, contributing to weight gain if fed often.
High starch content can affect blood glucose control.
May cause upset stomach, gas, or diarrhea.
High starch and fiber content may irritate sensitive bowels.

chia-seeds.reason: High fiber content can cause bloating, upset stomach, or diarrhea if not soaked or if overfed.
High fiber and potential for bloating may not be suitable during IBD flares; veterinarian guidance is recommended.

chicken-breast.reason: Chicken is a common dietary allergen for both dogs and cats.

chicken-broth.reason: Only plain, low-sodium broth is considered safe; regular or store-bought broths may contain too much sodium.
Sodium and additive content vary; pets with CKD require carefully controlled sodium intake.

chicken-gizzard.reason: Organ meat texture and richness may cause vomiting or diarrhea in sensitive pets.
Chewy texture may be difficult for pets with significant dental disease; cutting into small pieces is essential.

chicken-heart.reason: Poultry protein can trigger allergic reactions in pets with poultry allergies.
Rich organ meat may cause vomiting or diarrhea in sensitive pets.

chicken-liver.reason: Organ meats are high in purines, which can exacerbate disorders such as urate urolithiasis.
Nutrient-dense and calorie-rich; the file recommends feeding no more than 5% of the diet.
Organ meats are relatively rich and may not be ideal for pets with lipid disorders.
Rich organ meat can cause vomiting or diarrhea.

chicken-noodle-soup.reason: Commercial soups are usually high in salt, which is harmful for heart and hypertensive patients.
High sodium and potential onion/garlic content make it unsafe for pets with CKD.
Soups can be fatty and salty, increasing pancreatitis risk.
Noodles contribute simple carbohydrates that can disrupt glycemic control.
Onion, garlic, and seasonings commonly present can irritate the GI tract.
High salt and seasonings frequently cause vomiting or diarrhea.
May contain wheat (noodles) and chicken, common allergens.

chicken-skin.reason: High in fat and calories; the file states it is not a healthy treat.
High fat content can trigger pancreatitis; the file lists pancreatitis as a symptom.
Very high in fat, which can worsen hyperlipidemia.
Often seasoned with salt and spices that are unsafe for cardiac patients.
High fat content can contribute to insulin resistance and poor glycemic control.

chicken-thigh.reason: Higher in fat than breast meat; portion control is needed.
The file notes pancreatitis risk, especially if skin and fat are not trimmed.
Dark meat is higher in fat than lean breast.
Higher fat content can affect glycemic control compared to leaner proteins.

cinnamon.reason: Cassia cinnamon contains coumarin, and the file notes large amounts may harm the liver.
The file states large amounts may lower blood sugar, which is risky for diabetic pets on treatment.
Powder can irritate the mouth and GI tract, causing vomiting or diarrhea.
Irritant spice that can worsen GI inflammation.

cloves.reason: Contains eugenol, which can affect the liver in large amounts; cats are especially sensitive.
Eugenol can irritate the gut, causing drooling, vomiting, or diarrhea.
Strong irritant oil can aggravate inflamed bowels.

coconut-water.reason: The file explicitly states coconut water is high in potassium, which must be restricted in CKD.
High potassium content may interact with cardiac medications or require dietary adjustment.
May cause upset stomach or diarrhea.

coconut.reason: High fat content can trigger pancreatitis.
High fat content can worsen hyperlipidemia.
May cause diarrhea or upset stomach.

cod.reason: Fish is a potential allergen for some pets.

cookies.reason: High in sugar and fat, contributing to weight gain.
High sugar content can disrupt blood glucose control.
High fat content can trigger pancreatitis.
High fat and sugar content can worsen lipid disorders.
Sugary, sticky foods promote dental disease.
High sugar and fat commonly cause vomiting or diarrhea.
May contain wheat, dairy, eggs, or other common allergens.

coriander.reason: Even in small culinary amounts, some pets may experience mild stomach upset.

corn-on-the-cob.reason: High starch content can affect blood glucose.
May cause vomiting, constipation, or abdominal pain; cob obstruction is an additional hazard.
Starchy grain may irritate sensitive bowels.

corn-tortilla.reason: Corn is a potential allergen for some pets.
Mostly empty calories; the file says it should be fed rarely.
Carbohydrate-based food can affect glycemic control.

corn.reason: The file tags corn as a common allergen and advises monitoring for allergic reactions.
Starch content can affect blood glucose.

cottage-cheese.reason: Dairy product; although lower in lactose than milk, sensitive pets may still react.
Dairy protein and mineral content may need to be restricted in CKD; a veterinarian should approve.
Even low-fat varieties contribute calories; portion control is needed.

couscous.reason: The file states couscous is wheat-based and not suitable for dogs with wheat allergies.
Made from semolina wheat; high carbohydrate content can affect blood glucose.

cow-milk.reason: The file states most adult pets are lactose intolerant and may develop diarrhea, gas, bloating, or vomiting.
Whole milk is calorie-dense; the file recommends lactose-free or tiny amounts.
Contains lactose (milk sugar), which can affect glycemic control.
Dairy protein and phosphorus content are generally restricted in CKD.
Dairy is a common food allergen.

cranberries.reason: May cause upset stomach or diarrhea.

cream-cheese.reason: Dairy product that may cause digestive upset in lactose-intolerant pets.
High in fat and calories; the file states it is not suitable for overweight dogs.
High fat content may trigger pancreatitis; the file lists pancreatitis-risk as a symptom.
Very high in fat, inappropriate for hyperlipidemic pets.
High fat content can affect glycemic control.
May cause diarrhea, gas, or vomiting.

cream.reason: High in lactose and commonly causes diarrhea, vomiting, and bloating.
Very high in fat; the file notes pancreatitis risk.
Extremely high in fat and calories.
Very high fat content can worsen hyperlipidemia.
High fat and lactose sugar can disrupt glycemic control; the file explicitly says pets with diabetes should avoid cream.
The file explicitly states pets with sensitive stomachs should avoid cream.

cupcakes.reason: High in sugar and fat with no nutritional value.
High sugar content can cause blood glucose spikes.
High fat content may trigger pancreatitis.
High fat and sugar content can worsen lipid disorders.
Sugary, sticky frosting and cake promote dental disease.
High sugar and fat commonly cause vomiting or diarrhea.
May contain wheat, dairy, eggs, or other common allergens.

curry.reason: Often high in salt and may contain onion and garlic, which are unsafe for CKD patients.
High salt and fat content are contraindicated in cardiac disease.
Rich fats and spices increase pancreatitis risk.
Rich fats and carbohydrate bases can disrupt glycemic control.
Spices and rich fats can irritate the GI tract.
Onion, garlic, ginger, chili, and spices commonly cause vomiting and diarrhea.
Spice burden and potential allium content may stress the liver; severity-dependent.

dates.reason: Extremely high in sugar and calories; the file says avoid for overweight pets.
Very high sugar content can cause blood glucose spikes; the file specifically warns against feeding to diabetic pets.
Sticky, sugary fruit adheres to teeth and promotes dental disease.
High sugar can cause upset stomach or diarrhea.
High sugar intake can contribute to metabolic dysfunction.

deli-meat.reason: High in sodium, which is harmful for cardiac and hypertensive patients.
High sodium and preservatives are generally restricted in CKD.
Processed meat is calorie-dense and should be a rare treat.
Processed fat and sodium can aggravate pancreatitis.
Preservatives and high sodium may cause vomiting or diarrhea.
May contain additives, preservatives, or proteins that trigger sensitivities.

donuts.reason: Fried, high-sugar, high-fat food that promotes weight gain.
High sugar content can disrupt blood glucose control.
High fat content can trigger pancreatitis; the file lists pancreatitis as a symptom.
Very high fat and sugar content worsen lipid disorders.
Sugary, sticky coatings promote dental disease.
Fried fat and sugar commonly cause vomiting or diarrhea.

dragon-fruit.reason: Contains natural sugars; portion control is important for diabetic pets.
May cause upset stomach or diarrhea in sensitive pets.

dried-apricots.reason: Concentrated sugar and calories make dried apricots inappropriate for overweight pets.
Concentrated sugar content can cause blood glucose spikes.
Sticky, sugary dried fruit adheres to teeth and promotes dental disease.
High fiber and sugar can cause upset stomach or diarrhea.

duck.reason: Higher in fat than chicken breast; the file advises trimming skin and fat.
Fatty skin increases pancreatitis risk if not removed.
Dark poultry meat is fattier than lean whitefish or chicken breast.
Richness may cause vomiting or diarrhea in sensitive pets.

durian.reason: Very high in fat and sugar; the file says avoid for overweight pets.
High sugar content can disrupt blood glucose control.
High fat content increases pancreatitis risk; the file lists pancreatitis risk as a symptom.
Very high fat content is inappropriate for hyperlipidemic pets.
High fat and sugar commonly cause upset stomach or diarrhea.

edamame.reason: Soy is a potential allergen; the file advises introducing cautiously.
Legumes may be poorly tolerated during IBD flares.
Soy isoflavones may interfere with thyroid function; veterinary guidance is recommended for thyroid disorders.

eggplant.reason: Nightshade vegetable that can irritate the mouth and stomach, causing vomiting or diarrhea.
Potential GI irritant; raw eggplant is specifically not recommended.

eggs.reason: Eggs are a common food allergen for some pets.
Whole eggs contain fat in the yolk; moderation is advised for pets with lipid disorders.

elderberries.reason: Plant parts and unripe berries contain cyanogenic compounds that can cause severe vomiting, diarrhea, and weakness.

elk.reason: Rich game meat may cause vomiting or diarrhea in sensitive pets.

endive.reason: High fiber content can cause upset stomach or diarrhea.
High fiber may not be tolerated during IBD flares.

farro.reason: The file states farro contains gluten and should be avoided for gluten-sensitive dogs.
Wheat-based grain is high in carbohydrates.
May cause gas or mild diarrhea.
Gluten-containing grains may irritate sensitive bowels.

fennel.reason: May cause gas or mild diarrhea.
Fiber and aromatic oils may not be tolerated during flares.

figs.reason: May cause upset stomach, diarrhea, or skin irritation.
Fruit sugars and skin may irritate sensitive bowels.

flax-seeds.reason: High fiber and fat content can cause diarrhea or bloating.
Fiber and oil content may need to be adjusted during IBD management.

flour-tortilla.reason: The file states dogs with wheat allergies should avoid flour tortillas.
Higher in calories and fat than corn tortillas.
Wheat-based carbohydrate food can affect glycemic control.

french-fries.reason: Fried and calorie-dense; promotes weight gain.
High fat from frying oil can trigger pancreatitis; the file lists pancreatitis risk.
Very high fat content from frying is inappropriate for hyperlipidemic pets.
High in salt, which is harmful for cardiac and hypertensive patients.
High sodium content is generally restricted in CKD.
High fat and starchy potato can disrupt glycemic control.
Frying oil and salt commonly cause stomach upset or diarrhea.
High fat and fried foods can aggravate IBD.
